text: we present modular approach of total mesorectal excision for the trainees with robotic system the operation is divided into six modules as shown in this figure module one consists of patient setup force placement and exposure
The surgical team places the robotic ports in the designated positions on the patient's abdomen, preparing for the robotic-assisted total mesorectal excision.
The schematic illustration shows the placement of robotic ports R-I to R-IV, with an average distance of seven centimeters between each port.
The assistant port is visibly placed between the robotic ports R-III and R-IV, following the schematic layout of port placement.
The R-III port is designated as a stapling port, indicating its use for stapling instruments during the procedure.
The robotic port R-II serves as a camera port, providing a visual perspective that allows the surgeon to operate with two right-hand instruments.
In the surgical setup for obese patients, an additional 5mm assistant port is placed between the robotic ports R-I and R-II to optimize exposure.
The surgeon achieves a three-point exposure by displacing the small bowel medially within the right side of the peritoneal cavity, revealing the surgical field for further procedure.
The transverse colon and greater omentum are positioned cephalad, revealing an empty pelvis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a gauze piece to facilitate the current step in the procedure, demonstrating its application within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the duodenojejunal junction (DJ) and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) using a specific maneuver within the surgical field.
The robotic surgical system is docked from the left side of the patient, with the configuration set to focus on the left side and lower abdomen, following the exposure of the surgical area.
The surgeon performs ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), with the surgical field showing these vessels clearly exposed for the procedure.
The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, progressing towards the inferior mesenteric artery, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The robotic system provides a high-definition view of the surgical field, enhancing the visibility of anatomical structures and facilitating precise dissection.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect and skeletonize the fat surrounding the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), revealing the vessel clearly within the surgical field.
The robotic arms are visible, demonstrating their freedom of movement as they maneuver within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies anatomical structures in the surgical field, likely focusing on the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and vein (IMV) following their ligation.
The surgeon applies a vascular stapler to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) one centimeter distal to its origin, taking care to avoid injury to the nearby superior hypogastric nerve plexus.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near the duodenojejunal junction (DJ) using the robotic system, with clear visualization of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies, isolates, clips, and divides the structure at the inferior border of the pancreas, using robotic arms for precise manipulation.
The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization of the colorectal structures, utilizing robotic arms for enhanced precision and maneuverability.
The surgeon performs a superior dissection, continuing to mobilize the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully identifies the plane between the fasciae gerata and the mesocolon, ensuring precise dissection to avoid bleeding.
The surgeon uses the difference in color between tissues to aid in the dissection process, highlighting the visual contrast that assists in identifying anatomical planes.
The surgeon's left hand provides traction while the assistant's hand holds the fascia, applying counter traction to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon identifies the avascular plane between the fascia girota and the mesocolon, facilitating dissection in the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon continues superior dissection at the lower border of the pancreas, maintaining the avascular plane between the fascia girota and mesocolon.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the visible pancreas, taking precautions to avoid injury while continuing dissection in the avascular plane between the fascia girota and mesocolon.
A gauze piece is placed over the pancreas, covering the organ to protect it during the ongoing dissection.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, utilizing traction and counter traction to facilitate dissection in the avascular plane between the fascia girota and mesocolon.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection, visibly opening the peritoneum to reveal underlying structures.
The surgeon dissects inferiorly, revealing the ureters, which are identifiable by their visible peristaltic movements.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the ureter, iliac, and conodal vessels, ensuring these structures remain uninjured during the procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection and mobilizes the splenic flexure, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon initiates lateral dissection along the white line of Toldt, visibly separating the peritoneum from underlying structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection superiorly, following the previously opened peritoneum, while maintaining careful technique to avoid injury to nearby structures.
The assistant holds the left colon medially while the surgeon's left hand provides counter-traction, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure by detaching the greater omentum from the transverse colon, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon divides the ligaments around the splenic flexure while it is being pulled medially, facilitating further mobilization of the splenic flexure.
A gall space is visible, aiding in the identification of the correct surgical plane and providing protection to the pancreas during the procedure.
The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision (TME) and rectal transection, focusing on precise dissection and separation of rectal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon begins the posterior dissection of the colorectal region, with visible manipulation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon dissects between the pre-sacral fascia and the visceral fascia of the mesorectum, carefully navigating between the two hypogastric nerves.
The surgeon continues dissection in the holy plane between the pre-sacral fascia and the visceral fascia of the mesorectum, carefully avoiding injury to the pelvic nerves.

The surgeon continues the dissection laterally and then moves anteriorly, maintaining the holy plane between the pre-sacral fascia and the visceral fascia of the mesorectum, ensuring protection of the pelvic nerves.
The surgeon initiates anterior dissection in an inverse U-shaped manner, positioned just anterior to the non-validus fascia, as part of the oncological resection process.
The surgeon continues the dissection downwards, extending well below the tumor, maintaining the holy plane between the pre-sacral fascia and the visceral fascia of the mesorectum.
The seminal vesicles become visible during the dissection, and care is taken to avoid injury to these structures.
The surgeon dissects inferiorly, revealing the inner coccygeal ligament within the surgical field.

The surgeon cuts the inner coccygeal ligament under direct vision to achieve complete mobilization of the rectum. A rectal examination is performed to visually assess the tumor and determine the level of transection.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the rectum, completing the transection at the predetermined level.
The surgeon extracts the specimen and performs an anastomosis, completing the surgical procedure.
The surgeon displays the intact mesorectum, highlighting its preservation during the dissection process.
The surgeon exposes the left and right iliac vessels along with the pelvic nerves, highlighting their anatomical positions within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon displays the smooth surface of the pelvis, highlighting its intact and unblemished appearance.
The surgeon performs an endosannin green test to visually assess colonic perfusion, aiding in the decision for the level of resection and anastomosis.
The left alec vessels are illuminated by indocyanine green (ICG), highlighting the clonic perfusion in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a well-demarcated area within the surgical field, indicating clear boundaries or regions of interest.
Yellow and red arrows are used to indicate perfused and non-perfused sections of the colon, highlighting areas with adequate and inadequate blood supply.
The surgeon uses visual cues to establish a clear line of demarcation on the colon, indicating the level of resection based on perfusion assessment.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the resected ends of the colon.
